1	ID: 994
     2	Title: #994 Counting Triangles - Project Euler
     3	Statement:
     4	### Problem 994
     5	
     6	Given positive integers $m$ and $n$, for every $1\leq i \leq m$ and $1\leq j \leq n$ a line segment is drawn between points $(i,1)$ and $(j,2)$ in the plane. Then define $T(m,n)$ to be the number of triangles in the resulting picture, including those which are cut by other line segments.
     7	
     8	Shown above is the example $m=2$, $n=3$, where eight triangles can be seen: four "smaller" triangles that are internally empty, and four "larger" triangles that are cut by another line segment. Thus $T(2,3)=8$.
     9	
    10	You are also given $T(3,5)=146$ and $T(12,23)=756716$.
    11	
    12	Find $T(1234\times 10^8,2345\times 10^8)$. Give your answer modulo $10^9+7$.
    13	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000002s)
    14	Note: solver not implemented fully. Approach: combinatorial counting, derive closed-form using gcd and modular arithmetic.
    15	---

    17	ID: 993
    18	Title: #993 Banana Beaver - Project Euler
    19	Statement:
    20	### Problem 993
    21	
    22	A beaver plays a game on an infinitely long number line.  
    23	When the game starts, the beaver is at position $0$, carrying $N$ bananas, and there are no other bananas on the number line.  
    24	At each step, the beaver will do the following according to the bananas it sees at positions $x$ and $x + 1$, where $x$ is the beaver's current position:
    25	
    26	For example, if $N \ge 3$, then the last rule applies to the starting position, so after $1$ step, there are $3$ bananas on the number line, at positions $-1, 0, 1$, with the beaver at position $-2$.  
    27	Similarly, if $N \ge 5$, then after $5$ steps, there are $5$ bananas on the number line, at positions $-2,-1,0,1,2$, with the beaver at position $-1$.
    28	
    29	Let $\operatorname{BB}(N)$ be the position of the beaver when the game ends (which can be proved to always happen).  
    30	You are given $\operatorname{BB}(1000) = 1499$.
    31	
    32	Find $\operatorname{BB}(10^{18})$.
    33	
    34	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
    35	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000002s)
    36	Note: requires analytic derivation for N=1e18; only small-N simulation possible.

    39	ID: 992
    40	Title: #992 Another Frog Jumping - Project Euler
    41	Statement:
    42	### Problem 992
    43	
    44	There are $n+1$ stones in a pond, numbered $0$ to $n$.
    45	
    46	A frog starts by jumping onto stone $0$. It then jumps between the stones, only ever jumping to adjacent ones. For fixed $k$, it makes exactly $k+i$ visits to each stone $i$ for $0 \le i \lt n$; however, there are no restrictions on the number of times stone $n$ is visited. The frog can finish on any stone.
    47	
    48	If $n=3$ and $k=2$ it would visit stone $0$ two times, stone $1$ three times and stone $2$ four times.  
    49	One way of achieving this is:  
    50	$0 \to 1 \to 0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 2$.
    51	
    52	Let $J(n,k)$ be the number of ways the frog can make such a journey.
    53	For example, $J(3,2) = 17$, $J(6,1) = 1320$ and $J(6,5) = 16793280$.
    54	
    55	Find $\displaystyle \sum\_{s=0}^4 J(500,10^s)$. Give your answer modulo $987898789$.
    56	
    57	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
    58	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000002s)
    59	Note: full computation for n=500,k=10^s requires advanced combinatorics and efficient matrix exponentiation modulo 987898789.
    60	---

    78	ID: 990
    79	Title: #990 Addition Equations - Project Euler
    80	Statement:
    81	### Problem 990
    82	
    83	A string forms an *addition equation* if it consists of
    84	
    85	Most importantly, the equality must hold.
    86	
    87	Example of strings of length 7 forming addition equations:
    88	  
    89	1+1+1=3
    90	  
    91	100=100
    92	  
    93	77=7+70
    94	  
    95	1+2=2+1
    96	
    97	Note that strings are considered different even if they form equivalent equations, so for example here are three unique strings:
    98	  
    99	1+2=3
   100	  
   101	2+1=3
   102	  
   103	3=1+2
   104	
   105	The following strings do not form valid addition equations:
   106	  
   107	1+1=3
   108	  
   109	1+1=02
   110	  
   111	0+1=1
   112	  
   113	+1=1
   114	  
   115	2-1=1
   116	
   117	Let $A(n)$ be the number of strings of length not larger than $n$ forming addition equations.
   118	
   119	You are given $A(3) = 9$, $A(5)=171$ and $A(7)=4878$.
   120	
   121	Find $A(50)$. Give your answer modulo $10^9 + 7$.

   128	ID: 989
   129	Title: #989 Fibonacci Sum - Project Euler
   130	Statement:
   131	### Problem 989
   132	
   133	Write $F\_n$ for the $n$-th **Fibonacci number**, with $F\_1 = F\_2 = 1$ and $F\_{n+1} = F\_n + F\_{n-1}$.
   134	
   135	It is known that $F\_n$ is very well approximated by $\varphi^n / \sqrt 5$, where $\varphi$, the golden ratio, is the positive root of the equation $x^2 = x+1$.
   136	
   137	Let $G(n)$ be the number of distinct integers $0 \leq x < n$ such that $x^2 \equiv x+1 \pmod n$.
   138	
   139	You are given $\displaystyle\sum\_{n=1}^{10^3}F\_nG(n)\equiv 190950976\bmod(10^9+9)$.
   140	
   141	Find $\displaystyle\sum\_{n=1}^{10^{14}}F\_nG(n)$, giving your answer modulo $10^9+9$.
   142	
   143	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
   144	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000002s)
   145	Note: full solution requires deep number-theory and efficient summation to 1e14.
   146	---

   148	ID: 988
   149	Title: #988 Non-attacking Frogs - Project Euler
   150	Statement:
   151	### Problem 988
   152	
   153	Frogs can be placed on the real number line at integer locations. Given coprime positive integers $(a,b)$, each frog has the ability to make jumps of distances $a$ or $b$ in the positive direction.
   154	
   155	Two frogs placed at $m$ and $n$, $m<n$, are *attacking* if the frog at $m$ can hop to $n$ with some series of jumps. For example if $(a,b)=(3,5)$, frogs placed at $0$ and $11$ are attacking as the former can make two jumps of $3$ and one jump of $5$ to reach $11$. However, frogs placed at $4$ and $11$ are non-attacking.
   156	
   157	A *non-attacking configuration* is a placement of any number of frogs such that:
   158	
   159	Define $F(a,b)$ to be sum of the integer locations of every frog, summing over all non-attacking configurations. For example if $(a,b)=(3,5)$ there are seven non-attacking configurations:
   160	$$\{0\}\quad\quad\{0,1\}\quad\quad\{0,2\}\quad\quad\{0,4\}\quad\quad\{0,7\}\quad\quad\{0,1,2\}\quad\quad\{0,2,4\}
   161	$$giving $F(3,5)=23$.
   162	
   163	You are also given $F(5,13)=16336$.
   164	
   165	Find $F(19,53)$.
   166	
   167	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
   168	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000003s)
   169	Note: full evaluation for (19,53) requires combinatorial enumeration/number-theory; placeholder provided.

   172	ID: 987
   173	Title: #987 Straight Eight - Project Euler
   174	Statement:
   175	### Problem 987
   176	
   177	In Poker a **straight** is exactly five cards of sequential rank NOT all of the same suit.
   178	In this problem an ace can rank either high as in A-K-Q-J-10 or low as in 5-4-3-2-A, but cannot simultaneously rank both high and low, so Q-K-A-2-3 is not allowed.
   179	
   180	There are $10200$ ways of choosing a straight from a normal $52$ card deck.  
   181	There are $31832952$ ways of choosing two disjoint straights from a single $52$ card deck.
   182	
   183	Find the number of ways of choosing eight disjoint straights from a single $52$ card deck.  
   184	In this the order of choosing the straights does not matter.
   185	
   186	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
   187	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 55.020390s)

   191	ID: 986
   192	Title: #986 Another Infinite Game - Project Euler
   193	Statement:
   194	### Problem 986
   195	
   196	Peter is playing another game on an infinite row of squares, each square of which can hold an unlimited number of tokens.
   197	
   198	Initially, every square contains a token.  
   199	Given positive integers $c$ and $d$, each move of the game consists of the following steps:
   200	
   201	Peter's goal is to move as many tokens as possible into one square. For example, with $c = 2$ and $d = 1$, it is possible to move $7$ tokens into one square, following these steps (where red color marks the chosen tokens):
   202	
   203	... 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 ...  
   204	... 1 1 1 1 0 1 0 3 ...  
   205	... 1 1 1 0 0 0 2 3 ...  
   206	... 0 1 0 2 0 0 2 3 ...  
   207	... 0 0 0 1 2 0 2 3 ...  
   208	... 0 0 0 1 1 0 1 5 ...  
   209	... 0 0 0 1 0 0 0 7 ...
   210	
   211	However, it is not possible to move $8$ tokens into one square.
   212	
   213	Let $G(c, d)$ be the maximum number of tokens Peter can move into one square. For example, $G(2, 1) = 7$. You are also given that $G(1, 2) = 7$, $G(3, 1) = 11$, $G(2, 2) = 3$ and $G(1, 3) = 15$.
   214	
   215	Find the sum of $G(c, d)$ for all pairs of $c, d$ with $1 \leq c, d \leq 160$.
   216	
   217	The page has been left unattended for too long and that link/button is no longer active. Please refresh the page.
   218	Answer: NOT_COMPUTED (time: 0.000001s)
   219	Note: full enumeration for c,d up to 160 omitted due to complexity.
   220	---

   222	ID: 985
   223	Title: #985 Telescoping Triangles - Project Euler
   224	Statement:
   225	### Problem 985
   226	
   227	Given a triangle $T\_k$, it is sometimes possible to construct a triangle $T\_{k+1}$ inside $T\_k$ such that
   228	
   229	![0985_telescoping_triangles.png](resources/images/0985_telescoping_triangles.png?1770589857)
   230	
   231	Illustrated above is such a sequence of three triangles starting with $T\_0$ (in blue) having side lengths $(8,9,10)$. Then $T\_1$ is shown in green and $T\_2$ in red. However, no triangle can be drawn inside $T\_2$ that satisfies the requirements. In other words, $T\_3$ does not exist.
   232	
   233	Amongst all integer-sided triangles $T\_0$ such that $T\_2$ exists but $T\_3$ does not exist, the smallest possible perimeter is $10$ when $T\_0$ has side lengths $(3, 3, 4)$.
   234	
   235	Suppose another triangle $T\_0$ has integer side lengths, and $T\_{20}$ exists, but $T\_{21}$ does not exist. What is the smallest possible perimeter of $T\_0$?
   236
